findings.title: A hash algorithm used is considered weak and can cause hash collisions
findings.description: A hash algorithm used is considered weak and can cause hash collisions.
findings.recommendation: It is always recommended to use some CHF (Cryptographic Hash Function), which is mathematically strong and not reversible. SHA512 would be the most recommended hash for storing the password and it is also important to adopt some type of Salt, so that the Hash is more secure.

tool_specific.description: A hash algorithm used is considered weak and can cause hash collisions.
tool_specific.recomendation: It is always recommended to use some CHF (Cryptographic Hash Function), which is mathematically strong and not reversible. SHA512 would be the most recommended hash for storing the password and it is also important to adopt some type of Salt, so that the Hash is more secure.

findings.title: Using xml.etree.ElementTree to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.Elem
findings.description: Using xml.etree.ElementTree to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.ElementTree with the equivalent defusedxml package, or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called.

tool_specific.issue_text: Using xml.etree.ElementTree to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.ElementTree with the equivalent defusedxml package, or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called.

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.debug-enabled.debug-enabled
findings.description: Detected Flask app with debug=True. Do not deploy to production with this flag enabled as it will leak sensitive information. Instead, consider using Flask configuration variables or setting 'debug' using system environment variables.

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.directly-returned-format-string.directly-returned-format-string
findings.description: Detected Flask route directly returning a formatted string. This is subject to cross-site scripting if user input can reach the string. Consider using the template engine instead and rendering pages with 'render_template()'.
cwe_ids: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.code_excerpt:
return (
            "<form method='post'>"
            "<input name='username' placeholder='username'>"
            "<input name='password' placeholder='password' type='password'>"
            "<input type='hidden' name='next' value='%s'>"
            "<button type='submit'>Login</button>"
            "</form>" % (nxt.replace("'", "'"))
        )

tool_specific.lines:
return (
            "<form method='post'>"
            "<input name='username' placeholder='username'>"
            "<input name='password' placeholder='password' type='password'>"
            "<input type='hidden' name='next' value='%s'>"
            "<button type='submit'>Login</button>"
            "</form>" % (nxt.replace("'", "'"))
        )

findings.title: python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format.raw-html-format
findings.description: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.
cwe_ids: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.code_excerpt:
return (
            "<form method='post'>"
            "<input name='username' placeholder='username'>"
            "<input name='password' placeholder='password' type='password'>"
            "<input type='hidden' name='next' value='%s'>"
            "<button type='submit'>Login</button>"
            "</form>" % (nxt.replace("'", "'"))
        )

            "<form method='post'>"
            "<input name='username' placeholder='username'>"

            "<input type='hidden' name='next' value='%s'>"
            "<button type='submit'>Login</button>"
            "</form>" % (nxt.replace("'", "'"))
        )

findings.title: python.flask.security.injection.raw-html-concat.raw-html-format
findings.description: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`flask.render_template`) which will safely render HTML instead.
cwe_ids: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

findings.title: python.lang.security.deserialization.pickle.avoid-pickle
findings.description: Avoid using `pickle`, which is known to lead to code execution vulnerabilities. When unpickling, the serialized data could be manipulated to run arbitrary code. Instead, consider serializing the relevant data as JSON or a similar text-based serialization format.
owasp_categories: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures

findings.title: python.jwt.security.jwt-none-alg.jwt-python-none-alg
findings.description: Detected use of the 'none' algorithm in a JWT token. The 'none' algorithm assumes the integrity of the token has already been verified. This would allow a malicious actor to forge a JWT token that will automatically be verified. Do not explicitly use the 'none' algorithm. Instead, use an algorithm such as 'HS256'.
cwe_ids: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
findings.code_excerpt:
return jwt.decode(
                    token,
                    key=None,
                    algorithms=["none"],
                    audience=aud,
                    issuer=iss,
                    options=base_options,
                    leeway=leeway_eff,
                )

tool_specific.lines:
return jwt.decode(
                    token,
                    key=None,
                    algorithms=["none"],
                    audience=aud,
                    issuer=iss,
                    options=base_options,
                    leeway=leeway_eff,
                )

findings.title: javascript.lang.security.audit.path-traversal.path-join-resolve-traversal.path-join-resolve-traversal
findings.description: Detected possible user input going into a `path.join` or `path.resolve` function. This could possibly lead to a path traversal vulnerability,  where the attacker can access arbitrary files stored in the file system. Instead, be sure to sanitize or validate user input first.
cwe_ids: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.md5-used-as-password.md5-used-as-password
findings.description: It looks like MD5 is used as a password hash. MD5 is not considered a secure password hash because it can be cracked by an attacker in a short amount of time. Use a suitable password hashing function such as scrypt. You can use `hashlib.scrypt`.
cwe_ids: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

findings.title: python.lang.security.audit.md5-used-as-password.md5-used-as-password
findings.description: It looks like MD5 is used as a password hash. MD5 is not considered a secure password hash because it can be cracked by an attacker in a short amount of time. Use a suitable password hashing function such as scrypt. You can use `hashlib.scrypt`.
cwe_ids: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

findings.title: python.sqlalchemy.security.sqlalchemy-execute-raw-query.sqlalchemy-execute-raw-query
findings.description: Avoiding SQL string concatenation: untrusted input concatenated with raw SQL query can result in SQL Injection. In order to execute raw query safely, prepared statement should be used. SQLAlchemy provides TextualSQL to easily used prepared statement with named parameters. For complex SQL composition, use SQL Expression Language or Schema Definition Language. In most cases, SQLAlchemy ORM will be a better option.
cwe_ids: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

findings.title: python.sqlalchemy.security.sqlalchemy-execute-raw-query.sqlalchemy-execute-raw-query
findings.description: Avoiding SQL string concatenation: untrusted input concatenated with raw SQL query can result in SQL Injection. In order to execute raw query safely, prepared statement should be used. SQLAlchemy provides TextualSQL to easily used prepared statement with named parameters. For complex SQL composition, use SQL Expression Language or Schema Definition Language. In most cases, SQLAlchemy ORM will be a better option.
cwe_ids: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

findings.title: javascript.lang.security.audit.detect-non-literal-regexp.detect-non-literal-regexp
findings.description: RegExp() called with a `name` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.debug-enabled.debug-enabled
findings.description: Detected Flask app with debug=True. Do not deploy to production with this flag enabled as it will leak sensitive information. Instead, consider using Flask configuration variables or setting 'debug' using system environment variables.

findings.title: javascript.lang.security.audit.path-traversal.path-join-resolve-traversal.path-join-resolve-traversal
findings.description: Detected possible user input going into a `path.join` or `path.resolve` function. This could possibly lead to a path traversal vulnerability,  where the attacker can access arbitrary files stored in the file system. Instead, be sure to sanitize or validate user input first.
cwe_ids: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

findings.title: javascript.lang.security.audit.detect-non-literal-regexp.detect-non-literal-regexp
findings.description: RegExp() called with a `info` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.

findings.title: python.lang.security.use-defused-xml-parse.use-defused-xml-parse
findings.description: The native Python `xml` library is vulnerable to XML External Entity (XXE) attacks.  These attacks can leak confidential data and "XML bombs" can cause denial of service. Do not use this library to parse untrusted input. Instead  the Python documentation recommends using `defusedxml`.
cwe_ids: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

findings.title: javascript.express.security.audit.express-check-csurf-middleware-usage.express-check-csurf-middleware-usage
findings.description: A CSRF middleware was not detected in your express application. Ensure you are either using one such as `csurf` or `csrf` (see rule references) and/or you are properly doing CSRF validation in your routes with a token or cookies.

findings.title: javascript.express.security.audit.express-libxml-vm-noent.express-libxml-vm-noent
findings.description: Detected use of parseXml() function with the `noent` field set to `true`. This can lead to an XML External Entities (XXE) attack if untrusted data is passed into it.
cwe_ids: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.flask.security.audit.directly-returned-format-string
findings.description: Detected Flask route directly returning a formatted string. This is subject to cross-site scripting if user input can reach the string. Consider using the template engine instead and rendering pages with 'render_template()'.
cwe_ids: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.code_excerpt:
return (
            "<form method='post'>"
            "<input name='username' placeholder='username'>"
            "<input name='password' placeholder='password' type='password'>"
            "<input type='hidden' name='next' value='%s'>"
            "<button type='submit'>Login</button>"
            "</form>" % (nxt.replace("'", "'"))
        )

tool_specific.lines:
return (
            "<form method='post'>"
            "<input name='username' placeholder='username'>"
            "<input name='password' placeholder='password' type='password'>"
            "<input type='hidden' name='next' value='%s'>"
            "<button type='submit'>Login</button>"
            "</form>" % (nxt.replace("'", "'"))
        )

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.django.security.injection.raw-html-format
findings.description: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`django.shortcuts.render`) which will safely render HTML instead.
cwe_ids: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
findings.code_excerpt:
return (
            "<form method='post'>"
            "<input name='username' placeholder='username'>"
            "<input name='password' placeholder='password' type='password'>"
            "<input type='hidden' name='next' value='%s'>"
            "<button type='submit'>Login</button>"
            "</form>" % (nxt.replace("'", "'"))
        )

tool_specific.lines:
return (
            "<form method='post'>"
            "<input name='username' placeholder='username'>"
            "<input name='password' placeholder='password' type='password'>"
            "<input type='hidden' name='next' value='%s'>"
            "<button type='submit'>Login</button>"
            "</form>" % (nxt.replace("'", "'"))
        )

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.flask.security.injection.raw-html-format
findings.description: Detected user input flowing into a manually constructed HTML string. You may be accidentally bypassing secure methods of rendering HTML by manually constructing HTML and this could create a cross-site scripting vulnerability, which could let attackers steal sensitive user data. To be sure this is safe, check that the HTML is rendered safely. Otherwise, use templates (`flask.render_template`) which will safely render HTML instead.
cwe_ids: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.lang.security.audit.dangerous-subprocess-use-audit
findings.description: Detected subprocess function 'run' without a static string. If this data can be controlled by a malicious actor, it may be an instance of command injection. Audit the use of this call to ensure it is not controllable by an external resource. You may consider using 'shlex.escape()'.
cwe_ids: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
findings.code_excerpt:
p = subprocess.run(
        cmd,
        stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=subprocess.PIPE,
        text=True,
        timeout=timeout,
        shell=False,
    )

tool_specific.lines:
p = subprocess.run(
        cmd,
        stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=subprocess.PIPE,
        text=True,
        timeout=timeout,
        shell=False,
    )

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.lang.security.deserialization.avoid-pickle

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.jwt.security.jwt-python-none-alg
findings.description: Detected use of the 'none' algorithm in a JWT token. The 'none' algorithm assumes the integrity of the token has already been verified. This would allow a malicious actor to forge a JWT token that will automatically be verified. Do not explicitly use the 'none' algorithm. Instead, use an algorithm such as 'HS256'.
cwe_ids: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
findings.code_excerpt:
return jwt.decode(
                    token,
                    key=None,
                    algorithms=["none"],
                    audience=aud,
                    issuer=iss,
                    options=base_options,
                    leeway=leeway_eff,
                )

tool_specific.lines:
return jwt.decode(
                    token,
                    key=None,
                    algorithms=["none"],
                    audience=aud,
                    issuer=iss,
                    options=base_options,
                    leeway=leeway_eff,
                )

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.lang.security.insecure-hash-algorithm-md5
findings.description: Detected MD5 hash algorithm which is considered insecure. MD5 is not collision resistant and is therefore not suitable as a cryptographic signature. Use SHA256 or SHA3 instead.
cwe_ids: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.lang.security.audit.md5-used-as-password
findings.description: It looks like MD5 is used as a password hash. MD5 is not considered a secure password hash because it can be cracked by an attacker in a short amount of time. Use a suitable password hashing function such as scrypt. You can use `hashlib.scrypt`.
cwe_ids: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.sqlalchemy.security.sqlalchemy-execute-raw-query
findings.description: Avoiding SQL string concatenation: untrusted input concatenated with raw SQL query can result in SQL Injection. In order to execute raw query safely, prepared statement should be used. SQLAlchemy provides TextualSQL to easily used prepared statement with named parameters. For complex SQL composition, use SQL Expression Language or Schema Definition Language. In most cases, SQLAlchemy ORM will be a better option.
cwe_ids: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.sqlalchemy.security.sqlalchemy-execute-raw-query

For complex SQL composition, use SQL Expression Language or Schema Definition Language. In most cases, SQLAlchemy ORM will be a better option.
cwe_ids: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.javascript.lang.security.audit.detect-non-literal-regexp
findings.description: RegExp() called with a `name` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.

findings.description: Possibly found usage of AI: OpenAI

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.flask.security.audit.debug-enabled
findings.description: Detected Flask app with debug=True. Do not deploy to production with this flag enabled as it will leak sensitive information. Instead, consider using Flask configuration variables or setting 'debug' using system environment variables.

findings.description: Possibly found usage of AI: OpenAI

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.javascript.lang.best-practice.lazy-load-module
findings.description: Lazy loading can complicate code bundling if care is not taken, also `require`s are run synchronously by Node.js. If they are called from within a function, it may block other requests from being handled at a more critical time. The best practice is to `require` modules at the beginning of each file, before and outside of any functions.

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.javascript.lang.security.audit.path-traversal.path-join-resolve-traversal
findings.description: Detected possible user input going into a `path.join` or `path.resolve` function. This could possibly lead to a path traversal vulnerability,  where the attacker can access arbitrary files stored in the file system. Instead, be sure to sanitize or validate user input first.
cwe_ids: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.javascript.lang.security.audit.detect-non-literal-regexp
findings.description: RegExp() called with a `info` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.lang.security.use-defused-xml
findings.description: The Python documentation recommends using `defusedxml` instead of `xml` because the native Python `xml` library is vulnerable to XML External Entity (XXE) attacks. These attacks can leak confidential data and "XML bombs" can cause denial of service.
cwe_ids: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.python.lang.security.use-defused-xml-parse
findings.description: The native Python `xml` library is vulnerable to XML External Entity (XXE) attacks.  These attacks can leak confidential data and "XML bombs" can cause denial of service. Do not use this library to parse untrusted input. Instead  the Python documentation recommends using `defusedxml`.
cwe_ids: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.javascript.express.security.audit.express-check-csurf-middleware-usage
findings.description: A CSRF middleware was not detected in your express application. Ensure you are either using one such as `csurf` or `csrf` (see rule references) and/or you are properly doing CSRF validation in your routes with a token or cookies.

findings.title: app.opengrep-rules.javascript.express.security.audit.express-libxml-vm-noent
findings.description: Detected use of parseXml() function with the `noent` field set to `true`. This can lead to an XML External Entities (XXE) attack if untrusted data is passed into it.
cwe_ids: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference
